repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem
Using the `DiffTool` plugin (e.g. through `nvim -d ...` or `:DiffTool <file1> <file2>` fails if a space is in one of the paths. This occurs because `diff` wraps the paths with quotes (`'`) if space characters are present, which the line diff regex fails to parse.

## Solution
Update regex to handle quoted paths by matching the string within the quotes, if it exists.

### NOTE

Parsing can still fail for a variety of other reasons, such if the filename contains quotes (e.g. `john's application.txt`) or it contains the literal `and` (e.g. `kane and lynch.txt`).

Maybe some crazy regex could handle more cases, but before going to far down a rabbit-hole, just handling spaces like this probably addresses the majority of real-world issues (at least what I have run into). If you only want a more complete solution feel free to close this or let me know.

comment_data.body:
can we just add these extra files in the `setup()` and update the existing tests?  it looks like most of the rest of this test is redundant with the existing test(s), and there doesn't seem to be a reason to have an entirely separate, isolated test for this. 

there might be many more filename variants we want to test in the future, and adding 20 lines of new test code for each of those, would be cumbersome, while also missing out on the full coverage from the rest of these tests.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -49,6 +49,29 @@ describe('nvim.difftool', function()
     }, entries)
   end)
 
+  it('handles filenames containing spaces', function()
+    -- Create extra temp files with spaces in their name to test with.
+    local left = 'Xtest-difftool-space-left'
+    local right = 'Xtest-difftool-space-right'
+    n.mkdir_p(left)
+    n.mkdir_p(right)
+    finally(function()
+      n.rmdir(left)
+      n.rmdir(right)
+    end)
+    t.write_file(left .. pathsep .. 'file with space.txt', 'hello')
+    t.write_file(right .. pathsep .. 'file with space.txt', 'hello world')

comment_regular_data.body:
wouldnt something like this be a bit more robust? Because the quotes get matched with the pattern either way, the problem is that they are then included in filename. This ensures that it always matches the filenames, and then the sanitization can be done separately for different cases as well in case they pop up.

```lua
    local modified_left, modified_right = line:match('^Files (.+) and (.+) differ$')
    if modified_left and modified_right then
      local strip = "^'(.-)'$"
      modified_left = modified_left:match(strip) or modified_left
      modified_right = modified_right:match(strip) or modified_right
```

EDIT:

or slightly nicer with function and handle both ' and " at once

```lua
    local modified_left, modified_right = line:match('^Files (.+) and (.+) differ$')
    if modified_left and modified_right then
      local strip = function(s) return s:match("^'(.-)'$") or s:match('^"(.-)"$') or s end
      modified_left = strip(modified_left)
      modified_right = strip(modified_right)
```

> the sanitization can be done separately for different cases as well in case they pop up.

I think we should look at `diff`'s source code to see exactly how it escapes file names here, and then do the same in reverse. That way, we can guarantee that difftool can handle all possible file names, instead of making fixes as they appear.

> I think we should look at `diff`'s source code to see exactly how it escapes file names here

Gave a shot at looking for this (I would link it here but for some reason I can't load the source on git savannah so I had to unpack from the `dnf` package); looks like it calls this huge function for the file names:
```c
/* Place into buffer BUFFER (of size BUFFERSIZE) a quoted version of
   argument ARG (of size ARGSIZE), using QUOTING_STYLE, FLAGS, and
   QUOTE_THESE_TOO to control quoting.
   Terminate the output with a null character, and return the written
   size of the output, not counting the terminating null.
   If BUFFERSIZE is too small to store the output string, return the
   value that would have been returned had BUFFERSIZE been large enough.
   If ARGSIZE is SIZE_MAX, use the string length of the argument for ARGSIZE.

   This function acts like quotearg_buffer (BUFFER, BUFFERSIZE, ARG,
   ARGSIZE, O), except it breaks O into its component pieces and is
   not careful about errno.  */

static size_t

quotearg_buffer_restyled (char *buffer, size_t buffersize,
                          char const *arg, size_t argsize,
                          enum quoting_style quoting_style, int flags,
                          unsigned int const *quote_these_too,
                          char const *left_quote,
                          char const *right_quote)
...
```
It's called with the `shell_escape_quoting_style` option which additionally escapes backslashes. Though fully understanding what's going on might be a bit beyond me :sweat_smile: 

But I think it escapes most/all escape characters and may wrap the string in double quotes `"` instead of single quotes `'` if the given string itself has a single quote (e.g. `it's` -> `"it's"`). So that's at least one more case we could handle.

I can address the finding above and the existing issue using the strategy @ deathbeam proposed. Though if someone wants a different approach or take more time to look into `diff`'s implementation no worries.

> LGTM, why still in draft?

Sorry for the confusion and delay -- I meant to address the comments above but I've been getting hammered this week. I will have time to handle them/finish next week if that's alright and we still want those changes.
sure, though this PR is simple enough (after your revisions), so further improvements could be a followup

> sure, though this PR is simple enough, so further improvements could be a followup

oh yea cool works for me
